Clinical Empathy

David M. Berger. Northvale, NJ and London: Aronson, 1987, 294 pp., $30.00.

John Suler PHD

John Suler

John Suler   Is an Associate Professor of Psychology at Rider College and in private practice in Doylestown, PA. His publications include research on mental imagery in psychoanalytic therapy, creativity, and the integration of psychoanalytic theory with Eastern thought.

Few people would deny that empathy always has been an important aspect of psychoanalytic treatment. However, as Berger points out in Clinical Empathy, discussions in the literature about this topic have increased dramatically over the past 10 years. To a large extent, this rising interest is the result of Kohut's emphasis on empathy as both the essential healing factor and cornerstone of data collection in psychoanalysis.

In the first section of his book, in which he reviews concepts of empathy, Berger agrees that empathy facilitates the healing process and data collection, but he does not emphasize these ideas as strongly as the self psychologists; nor does he endorse other self psychological views about empathy. He only briefly discusses the role of empathy in the strengthening of self-structure through the analysis of selfobject transferences and transmuting internalizations. He does not agree that empathy occurs when the patient “feels understood” or that pathology necessarily stems from a lack of parental empathy in childhood. He does not believe that empathy is “warmth” and “acceptance,” a view often attributed to self psychology. Instead, he assumes a broader, more traditional perspective, maintaining that “the term empathy should be used only to denote experientially knowing another person's inner state” (p. 228). It consists of an emotional component, which is the clinician's personal and theoryless reactions to the patient (similar to the self psychological concept of empathy as “experience near”), and an intellectual component, which is derived from the theories that guide and give meaning to the clinician's emotional experience (this component seems to violate Berger's definition of empathy as “experientially knowing”). In the psychoanalytic context, empathy is used to promote growth; but in other situations, according to Berger, it could be used to manipulate and hurt people.

responseSet.title: Comments on Norman Holland's Book Review of The Works of Jacques Lacan: An Introduction, by Bice Benvenuto and Roger Kennedy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comments on Norman Holland's Book Review of The Works of Jacques Lacan

An Introduction, by Bice Benvenuto and Roger Kennedy

Gerard Pape PHD

Gerard Pape

Ann Arbor, MI

Gerard Pape   Is in private practice in Ann Arbor, MI. He is a consultant to the Jackson County Department of Social Services, specializing in cases of child abuse and neglect. He has studied and written several articles on the psychoanalytic contributions of Jacques Lacan, and is co-founder of the Ann Arbor Lacan Study Group.

This article comments on Holland's (1990) review of Benvenuto and Kennedy's 1986 work, The Works of Jacques Lacan: An Introduction, contending that Holland dismisses Lacan's theory without taking into account Lacan's later concept of the Real, and that the review presents an oversimplified, misinformed view of Lacan's other concepts, such as the Symbolic and the Imaginary. It is not only language that is crucial for its psychic impact for Lacan, but also traumatic events which have their effects (the Real), the way unconscious family and cultural laws and expectations are signified (the Symbolic), and the way ideal images of self and other create ego fictions (the Imaginary). Some of Lacan's contributions to developmental theory and to the critique of ego psychology and object relations theory are discussed. I disagree with Holland's conclusion that Lacan's theory is antipsychoanalytic and that Lacanian treatment is reduced to word games.

[[RunningHead]]

Knowledge as Desire: An Essay on Freud and Piaget

Hans G. Furth. New York: Columbia University Press, 1987, 179 pp., $22.50

Gary Fireman PHD

Gary Fireman

Gary Fireman   Is an Assistant Professor in the Graduate Department of Clinical Psychology at Texas Tech University. His current interests include cognitive development, theoretical systems, and child psychopathology. His empirical research is on the development of conceptualization, reflective awareness, and perspective taking in various problem situations.

Two critical questions for a science of mind are why we think and feel the way we do and how we come to think and feel this way. The question of “why” concerns the psychological subject and the individual subject. Often framed in biological terms, the emphasis is on individual differences, abnormal behavior, and personality. The question of “how” concerns the epistemic subject. Here the emphasis is on the general structures of mind and the development of knowledge. In this arena, questions of the relation between knowledge and emotion are critical. That is, through the synthesis of knowledge and emotion, the gap in understanding between personal everyday experiences and general scientific theory may be closed. The synthesis of knowledge and emotion is the primary goal of H. G. Furth's book, Knowledge as Desire: An Essay on Freud and Piaget. Taking Freud as having provided the most comprehensive theory of emotion, and Piaget as having provided the most comprehensive theory of knowledge, Furth states, “What is lacking, however, for a fruitful understanding of either theory is a whole picture that directly links knowledge and emotions” (p. 3). He interprets Freud and Piaget as splitting intellect and emotion into separate and possibly antagonistic processes, and argues that a synthesis of Píaget's theory of knowledge and Freud's theory of drive will produce a more complete theory of mind.

Furth's Intention in Knowledge as Desire

Furth begins by noting the different foci of Freud and Piager, then sketches some basic similarities between Freud's and Piaget's conceptions of mind.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Previewing

A Developmental Phenomenon and a Therapeutic Strategy

Paul Trad MD

Paul Trad

Paul Trad   Is an Assistant Professor of Psychiatry at Cornell University Medical Center, New York Hospital; Director of the Child and Adolescent Outpatient Department at New York Hospital-Westchester Division; and a certified analyst. He has written several books in his area of expertise, developmental psychopathology in infancy and the preschool years. His most recent book is entitled, Infant Previewing: Predicting and Sharing Interpersonal Outcome.

Previewing, a phenomenon that occurs within the caregiver-infant relationship, enhances the infant's awareness of future interpersonal behavior patterns and the infant's capacity to regulate stimuli from internal and external sources. Previewing—for example, the caregiver's manipulation of the infant's limbs to stimulate the movements of crawling behavior in order to expose the infant to the experience of that milestone in its consolidated form—integrates the analogic mode of communication (behavioral replications of the previewed experience) with the digital mode of communication (symbolic replications of the previewed experience), and results in the sharing of developmental changes within the dyad. Moreover, previewing behaviors help explain the nature of the evolving relationship between caregiver and infant and the processes whereby expectations concerning the future are generated.

New research is using previewing as a therapeutic technique with adult patients, in order to transcend the limitations posed by traditional analytic communication, to predict psychological conflict, and to overcome the communication, to predict psychological conflict, and to overcome the communication deficits that often occur with conventional techniques which rely primarily on retrospective inquiry and narrative reconstruction by the patient. Previewing exercises can also foster the transference relationship between analyst and patient, and can promote the emergence of a mastery orientation that will endure after the treatment is terminated. This article addresses the scope of previewing as a psychotherapeutic technique for promoting adaptive change.

A Rejoinder to Gerard J. Pape's Comments

Norman Holland PHD

Norman Holland

Norman Holland   Is the Marston-Milbauer Professor of English at the University of Florida, and the author of nine books and over 100 articles applying psychoanalysis to literary problems.

Pape's (1991) clear paraphrases only demonstrate again two problems with Lacan's work. First, Lacan's obscurity leads Pape and others to an explication of what is being said instead of assessing the value of what is being said. Second, key concepts, such as “the Real” rest on a pre-psychoanlytic idea of psychic causality. For Lacan, words, events, or traumata cause symptoms directly, unmediated by defense. This idea Freud rejected with the “seduction hypothesis.” Lacan's mistake about “the Real” parallels his mistakes about “the Symbolic.” His linguistic thinking about the Symbolic rests on Saussure's behaviorist idea of mental functioning as “signification.” His ideas about the Real likewise rest on a pre-psychoanalytic idea of unmediated causality from external event to internal.

Call for Papers

Psychoanalytic Psychology announces a call for papers for a supplement to the Journal regarding psychoanalytic perspectives on the issue of Aggression and Violence. The Journal welcomes theoretical, clinical, and empirical papers reporting on the value of psychoanalytic perspectives in understanding the origins and resolution of conflict. While contributions may address the general issue of violence and aggression, papers focusing on psychoanalytic contributions to the study of international violence and war will be particularly welcomed. Please send four copies of double-spaced manuscripts, in APA style, to Bertram J. Cohler, Editor, Psychoanalytic Psychology, The University of Chicago, 5730 South Woodlawn Avenue, Chicago, IL 60637. Please note on the outside envelope and on the manuscript that the manuscript is submitted in response to the call for papers on conflict. The regular review process for the Journal applies to this special supplement. Manuscripts should be submitted by January 1, 1992.

Image and Action

On the Link Between Subjective Representation and Interpersonal Relations

Alan Grey PHD and Anthony Losardo PHD

Anthony Losardo

Anthony Losardo   Serves as a consultant to the New York City Board of Education concerning children with severe psychological problems. He is in private practice in Brooklyn, NY.

A tenet widely accepted in psychoanalytic thought is that the relative maturity of subjective representations of significant others indicates the relative maturity of actual behavior with others. To test this, 120 normal seventh-grade boys and girls at one New York City school were studied, using two measures of interpersonal maturity. One was the Object Representation Scale for Dreams (Krohn, 1972), a device for rating maturity level in dreams. The other was the Interpersonal Maturity Nominating Technique (Losardo, 1983), a sociometric assessment of observed level of social participation, as judged by the peer group, using maturity criteria of Mahler, Pine, and Bergman (1975). The data confirmed an association between the two measures. The data also were used to test whether the more mature group had reached a level of heterophilic intimacy expected by classical theory but not by interactional theory. Results favored the latter theory.

A Classification of Empathic Failures for Psychotherapists and Supervisors

Edna Mordecai PHD

Edna Mordecai

Edna Mordecai   Is in private practice in Wayland, MA and is a member of the supervising faculty of the Boston Institute for Psychotherapy, Boston. She is also a Research Associate at the Institute where she is studying the processes and outcomes of long-term psychotherapy.

Empathic failure is a normal characteristic of all human relationships. In psychotherapy and the supervision of psychotherapy, empathic failures can have a negative effect; however, with appropriate management, these failures can represent an important opportunity for a therapeutic experience. Due to a variety of factors embedded in the transference/countertransference ambience of therapy, empathic failures are often obscured and neglected by therapists and supervisors. This article proposes a classification for six types of empathic failures according to their sources. It suggests the range of failures within each type, and specifies the obscuring factors, the common errors in response, and the possible interventions for each type of empathic failure.

Dreams of Love and Fateful Encounters: The Power of Romantic Passion

Ethel Spector Person. New York: Norton, 1988, 384 pp., $18.95

Jeanne Safer PHD

Jeanne Safer

Jeanne Safer   Is Supervisor and Faculty, Postgraduate Center for Mental Health and National Institute for the Psychotherapies. Her current interests include intersubjective aspects of dream interpretion and maternal transference/countertransference in the treatment of victims of paternal incest.

Dreams of Love and Fateful Encounters has one of the most intriguing titles of any recent psychoanalytically informed book. Rarely does a respected researcher-clinician address the topic of love in such unshamedly terms; analytic forays into this territory tend to emphasize etiology or consequences of intense affect states, rather than attempting to depict the states themselves as subjectively experienced. That task has been principally the province of poetry and fiction on the one hand and self-help or confessional pulp on the other.

It is Person's intention to write a natural history and encyclopedia of romantic love, both heterosexual and homosexual, in all its vicissitudes. Eschewing jargon, she charts love's permutations (including seduction, flirtation, and three different kinds of triangles), its childhood origins, idyllic beginnings, inevitable conflicts, and various denouements from disillusionment, betrayal, and loss to sustaining joy. Person attributes to psychoanalysis an undue, reductionistic emphasis on sexuality and psychopathology which has narrowed the focus of inquiry; her own definition of romance and passion, while including the erotic component, is not confined to this. She gives prominence to both the “gold mine and the mine field” (p. 254)—the potential for self-transformation as well as for devastation in the abandonment of self and partial merger that are fundamental features of passion.

Central to Person's approach is the notion of love as a catalyst for change; she sees passion as a “paradigm for any significant realignment of personality and values” (p. 23). It is progressive as well as regressive. In addition to an ecstatic rediscovery of symbiotic merger, “the glory of love is that it also moves us through the past and out of it” (p. 129).

[[RunningHead]]

The Resistance Scale

Background and Psychometric Properties

Ronna Schuller PHD , Paul Crits-Christoph PHD and Mary Connolly BA

Mary Connolly

Mary Connolly   is currently a graduate student in clinical psychology at Vanderbilt University. Her interests are in research on psycholdynamic psychotherapy.

The purpose of this study was to develop and examine a scale for measuring the type and quantity of resistance in dynamic psychotherapy. We present theoretical background and psychometric characteristics of the rating scale. The 19-item Resistance Scale was rated by clinical judges on verbatim excerpts from therapy sessions. Results indicated that resistance is a multidimensional construct. Principal components analysis revealed four resistance subtypes: Abrupt/Shifting, Flat/Halting, Oppositional, and Vague/Doubting. Analysis of variables affecting scale variance revealed that the scale measures not only episodic resistance, but to a certain extent stable patient characteristics as well.

[[RunningHead]]

Immaterial Facts: Freud's Discovery of Psychic Reality and Klein's Development of His Work

Robert Caper. Northvale, NJ: Aronson, 1988, xvii + 266 pp., '25.00.

Harold Gerard PHD

Harold Gerard

Harold Gerard   Is a Professor of Psychology at UCLA. He is an experimental social psychologist with analytic training, currently conducting laboratory research on the vicissitudes of unconscious processes.

Although Melanie Klein's concept of projective identification has gradually entered the mainstream of psychoanalysis (e.g., Grotstein, 1981; Kernberg, 1987; Meissner, 1987; Ogden, 1982; Sandler, 1987), her theory of mental development within which projective identification is the key mechanism, is still, in many quarters, considered heretical. Projective identification as a process has, by and large, been lifted out of its basic developmental context.

Besides the concrete and phantasmagorical terms in which the theory is couched, which has a certain off-putting quality, there have been other, more political reasons why the acceptance of Klein's ideas has been, at times, fiercely resisted. For one thing she was a woman; for another she had absolutely no academic credentials, having had no formal training beyond high school, the Austrian gymnasium. In 1914 she happened to read On Dreams, Freud's (1901) abbreviated version of his dream book. She knew then what she wanted to do as a life's work. As was the case in the early days, and still is to some extent, she received her training informally, mostly through two brief analyses, one with Sandor Ferenczi and the other with Karl Abraham. By the mid-1920s, her new ideas were raising hackles in Berlin. After her analyst, protector, and champion, Abraham, died in 1924, her remaining in Berlin became all but untenable. With considerable help from Alix and James Strachey, Freud's translators, she was invited in 1925 to give a series of lectures on child analysis at the British psychoanalytic Institute. The lectures were very well received, and she was subsequently asked to join the Institute, which she did in 1926.

By the mid-1930s, Klein had established herself as the leading intellectual figure in the British Institute.

Women and Depression: A Lifespan Perspective, edited by Ruth Formanek and Anita Gurian. New York: Springer, 1988, 306 pp., $31.95.

Women and Depression: A Lifespan Perspective, an anthology by Ruth Formanek and Anita Gurian, is a string of cultured pearls. The editors have gathered a group of women and men, generally of outstanding training and experience, who have in turn captured with great sensitivity the vicissitudes of women's experiences over the life span. Risking cliché, we see women as human beings here, not vessels of chromosomes, chemicals, or hormones. There are few mentions of tranquilizers or antidepressants. In the same vein, no simple-minded remedies or holistic quick fixes are offered. Instead, women are afforded the dignity of feeling and behaving appropriately to personal and social adversities. The tone for this is set and set straight by Formanek and Gurian in their introductory remarks:

Aristotle, alluding to cyclothymic disorders, believed that black bile, which is cold by nature, can produce paralysis, depression or anxiety states. However, … suspicion of a relationship between depression and physiological functioning continues in present-day psychiatry, although gall, bile, and slime have now been ruled out in favor of neurotransmitters [italics added]. (p. xiv)

NOTE: This is Part III of a three-part essay that reviews six books on psychoanalysis and women.

[[RunningHead]]

The Said and Unsaid of Self Psychology, Part I

The Question of Language

Lucia Villela-Minnerly PHD

Lucia Villela-Minnerly

Lucia Villela-Minnerly   Teaches at Chicago State University and at the Center for Psychoanalytic Study, and she is in private practice in Chicago. Her current interests are psychoanalytic theories, the psychoanalytic analysis of culture (especially films), and theories of development.

In his article on introspection and empathy, Kohut (1959) defined psychoanalysis in terms of methods used rather than tenets held, and thus started a far reaching conceptual revolution that has considerably modified and expanded Freud's drive-based, psychosexual model. Over the last 30 years, a more-or-less cohesive system of explanatory propositions has developed into what we now call self psychology. The said of self psychology will examine some of the explicitly stated propositions for their conceptual coherence. The unsaid will examine some of the covert or seldom articulated questions and assumptions that guide (or that subvert) the self psychological approach. It is argued that language (examined here) and sexuality (examined in a later article) are two of the major unsaids of self psychology. This article assumes that all theories have their unsaids, and therefore the subversion of a theory may be a precondition of its contribution to future work.

Freud: A Life For Our Time

Peter Gay. New York: Norton, 1988, xx + 810 pp., $30.00

Harold Fine PHD and Bradley Collins M. Phil.

Bradley Collins

Bradley Collins   Is a doctoral candidate in art history at Columbia University. He is writing a dissertation on Psychoanalytic Biography and Art History. He has taught at Hunter College and Parsons School of Design and has written for Art Journal, Art in America, and The Village Voice.

Part 1. Freud: A Time For a Life by Harold J. Fine

At best, psychoanalysis is structured and detached autobiography. Gay's biography of Freud was one of the most moving reading experiences I've had in a long time. Perhaps the boundaries of projective identification were loosened as I took up the task of an examined life in Freud's Vienna, his East European roots, and, most of all, Freud the Jew—Eine Jude. How and why did a Godless Jew discover psychoanalysis? Perhaps the answer concerns his marginality—a Jew examining his inner and outer being during the declining Habsburg Empire where he saw, felt, and raged at a feudal and bourgeois culture that had repression at its core, hypocrisy at its roots, and anti-Semitism as its perverse élan vital. Austria was slouching toward Nazism, Vienna was becoming one of the graveyards of Europe—this despite a renaissance in the arts and sciences which was superficially liberal at best. Freud, ever the realist or pessimist, early sensed this destructive course, first in his rage and humiliation when his father's hat was knocked off by an anti-Semite (Freud was 8 years old), and then in his ironic comment about the Gestapo in 1938 when he was ransomed to England, where he spent the last 112 years of his life: “I can most highly recommend the Gestapo to everyone” (p. 628).

responseSet.title: The Feminine Unconscious and Psychoanalytic Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Feminine Unconscious and Psychoanalytic Theory

Ellen Toronto PHD

Ellen Toronto

Ellen Toronto   Is in private practice in Ann Arbor, MI. Her current intellectual interests center on the concept of primary femininity and female sexual development.

A primary feminine experience is presupposed. Our collective resistance to knowledge about such an experience is seen as arising out of a patriarchal world view which defines man as subject and woman either as maternal object of the child or sexual object of the man. The article addresses the impact of patriarchy and, particularly, male sexual aggression, on the developing female. The problems of collecting data about the femine unconscious, given the male bias of our theories, are also discussed. Finally, early and modern theories about the primary feminine experience are presented along with illustrative clinical material. Emphasis is placed on woman's reproductive and relational capabilities as well as on her development as a sexual individual, arising out of early and ongoing vaginal awareness.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Child Sexual Abuse

A Review of the Post-Freudian Literature

Elizabeth Wolf MA and Judith Alpert PHD

Judith Alpert

Judith Alpert   Is president of Section III (Women and Psychoanalysis) of Division 39, American Psychological Association; Professor of the Doctoral Programs in School Psychology and Professional Child/School Psychology at New York University; and practicing psychoanalyst in New York City. She edited Psychoanalysis and Women: Contemporary Reappraisals (The Analytic Press, 1986; German edition: Springer-Verlag, 1991). Her current intellectual interests are amnesia and analytic reconstruction in the treatment of incest survivors.

This article reviews the post-Freudian psychoanalytic literature that focuses on child sexual abuse. Most of the articles reviewed are case presentations; there is little new theoretical work and few empirical studies. The present review focuses on the nature and effects of abuse; the parental dynamics in incestuous families; treatment issues and outcomes; and theory. The review results in the following observations: (a) Father-daughter incest is the form of abuse most frequently indicated in these articles; (b)little literature exists on the abuse of boys; (c) the effects of sexual abuse are often serious and enduring; (d) little literature exists on treatment issues, specifically whether and how analysis should be modified with sexually abused population; (e) there is no consensus about what is curative; and (f) treatment outcomes vary widely. Although contributions to the psychoanalytic literature on child sexual abuse have increased dramatically in the last 10 years, further investigation is indicated. The article concludes with a discussion of some areas for additional work.

The Misuse of Countertransference Love in Sexual Intimacies Between Therapists and Patients

Andrea Celenza PHD

Andrea Celenza

Andrea Celenza   Is the Associate Director of Psychology at The Cambridge Hospital, Harvard Medical School. She is also a candidate at the Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. Dr. Celenza is currently interested in supervision and training issues, especially as they relate to the establishment of boundaries, the capacity for empathy, and transference/countertransference dilemmas. She is in private practice in Cambridge, MA.

This article presents theoretical considerations and case material from the ongoing psychotherapy of an offending therapist whose license was suspended for having sexual relations with a patient. Problems of inadequate training and mistaking the transference as real are deemed important factors contributing to the abuse. However, most important was the offending therapist's misuse of his countertransference love to circumvent the patient's transference rage and hate. The therapist's narcissistic vulnerabilities (including early sexualization of pregenital needs and grandiose compensatory rescue fantasies) served as unconscious motivating factors for the acting out while his problematic relationship to authority figures rendered the transgression relatively ego-syntonic. Implications for supervision and training of psychotherapists are discussed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rapprochement or Approchement

Mahler's Theory Reconsidered From the Vantage Point of Recent Research on Early Attachment Relationships

Karlen Lyons-Ruth PHD

Karlen Lyons-Ruth

Karlen Lyons-Ruth   Is an Assistant Professor at Harvard Medical School and a supervising psychologist in the Department of Psychiatry at the Cambridge Hospital. Her research and theoretical interests center on exploring the organization of internalized relational models during infancy and early childhood, including associated processes of developmental and therapeutic change.

Mahler speculated that the normal developmental process of separation-individuation led young toddlers to experience ambivalence when needing comforting contact with a caregiver. She labeled this period of characteristic ambivalence the rapprochement subphase of the separation-individuation process, roughly spanning the developmental period from 15 to 24 months of age. Researchers observing mother–infant relationships from an attachment perspective have also noted the ambivalent behaviors described by Mahler, as well as a range of other conflict behaviors that become increasingly prominent after 12 months of age. However, recent work on attachment relationships indicates that ambivalent behavior and other forms of conflict behavior centered around the need for comforting contact with mother in early toddlerhood are more likely to be related to difficulties in parent-infant interaction than to normative ambivalence related to a “fear of reengulfment,” with more conspicuous forms of infant conflict related to the presence of maternal psychopathology. The attachment literature leading to this conclusion is reviewed, including new work on disorganized/disoriented infant attachment behaviors, and recent longitudinal studies of the sequelae of early attachment patterns through age 6. Revisions in the existing framework of object-relations theory are proposed to encompass the new developmental data.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Training Analysis and Training Psychotherapy

Moss Rawn PHD

Moss Rawn

Moss Rawn   Is past President, Training and Supervising Analyst, and faculty member of the New York Freudian Society; training and supervising analyst and faculty member of the National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis; and member of the International Psychoanalytical Association. His current intellectual interests are psychoanalytic technique and process, philosophy of science in psychoanalysis, and psychoanalytic training.

Psychoanalysis, training analysis, psychotherapy, and training psychotherapy are defined. The following questions are addressed: (a) How does training analysis differ from ordinary analysis?; (b) Should a different type of training analysis be required for psychotherapy (i.e., psychoanalytic psychotherapy) candidates as contrasted to psychoanalytic candidates?; and (c) What is the effect on the training analysis of recent changes in psychoanalytic theory and technique?

The effects on training analysis of three different clinical stances (ego psychology, object relations, and self psychology) are described. Composite case examples are constructed to illustrate the main themes. The basic position taken is that there is no substitute for a full, thorough training analysis for all psychoanalytic and psychoanalytic psychotherapy candidates. A training psychotherapy is never indicated.

Attachment Patterns and Freudian Theory

An Integrative Proposal

Doris Silverman PHD

Doris Silverman

Doris Silverman   Is Adjunct Professor in the doctoral and postdoctoral programs, New York University; faculty member and training analyst, IPTAR (Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research); and member of the editorial boards of Psychoanalytic Psychology and Psychoanalytic Dialogues: A Journal of Relational Perspectives. She is interested in infant research and its implications for psychoanalytic theories.

Freud and subsequent analysts have described the infants’ proximity, safety, and security needs and caregivers’ responses to them. In this article, they are discussed as activating an attachment system, a nonlibidinally organized pattern of interactions between infants and their significant figures that becomes structuralized. Attachment theory and research are presented. An attempt is made to integrate this body of work into Freudian theory utilizing the concept of the self-preservative instinces, thereby providing an explicit object relations motivational system equal to drives. Clinical examples are presented that demonstrate the importance of attachment relationships, and illustrate the interweaving of drive-defense configurations and object relations.

Reclaiming the Metapsychology

Classical Revisionism, Seduction, and the Self in Freudian Psychoanalysis

Steven Reisner PHD

Steven Reisner

Steven Reisner   Is Director of Psychology at the Regent Hospital, New York and Adjunct Assistant Professor in Psychology and Education at Teachers College, Columbia University. His current intellectual interests include Freud's theory as it departs from “Freudian” theory, the interplay of creativity and transference, and the vicissitudes of the death drive in addictive and compulsive disorders.

Contemporary psychoanalytic theory is in conflict as to what constitutes its basic motivational assumptions. Classical Freudian metapsychology has been challenged by self and relational theorists as outdatedly “physicochemical” (Schafer, 1976). In this article, it is argued that the metapsychology being debated in the literature is not Freud's, but a narrowed and selective representation of Freud's theory, as codified by his followers. It is further argued that in its classical representation the history of the development of Freud's theories has also been distorted in order to support this selective view of Freud's metapsychological theory. Two examples are offered in which Freud's own writings are contrasted with the classical representation of those writings. It is proposed that what has been excised is precisely that aspect of Freud's work which is compatible with the seemingly contradictory views offered by contemporary theorists, and that a resurrection of “the Witch Metapsychology” (Freud, 1937) may provide a creative, integrative foundation for psychoanalytic theory building.

There is nothing for it but to ‘summon help from the Witch’—the Witch Metapsychology. Without metapsychological speculation and theorizing—I had almost said ‘phantasy’—we shall not get a step further. (Freud, 1937, p. 326)

[[RunningHead]]

Starved for Words

On the Anorexia of Language

Nancy Burke PHD

Nancy Burke

Nancy Burke   Is a recent graduate of The University of Chicago, Committee on Human Development, and is currently a post-doctoral fellow at The Institute of Psychiatry, Northwestern Memorial Hospital. She is interested in pursuing the study of psychoanalysis in an interdisciplinary context, with a particular focus on the psychology of women.

Observers of the distinctive modes of communication employed by young women suffering from anorexia nervosa tend to view the form and content of the language these young women use as a reflection of their deviant approach to food and eating. In this article, however, it is suggested that we turn our attention from the issue of how anorexic individuals communicate to the question of how certain individuals, no doubt eating-disordered women among them, use words themselves anorexically. The concept of an anorexia of language is illustrated by two case studies, one focusing on an 18-year-old woman manifesting a plurality of forms of anorexia, and another describing a 22-year-old woman whose anorexia manifested itself primarily in her use of language.

[[RunningHead]]

Object-Choice Revisited

Alan Blum PHD and Carolyn Shadduck PHD

Carolyn Shadduck

Los Angeles

Carolyn Shadduck   Is a member of the Faculty, Los Angeles Institute and Society for Psychoanalytic Studies. Her intellectual interests include psychoanalytic theories of development and early object loss.

The psychoanalytic concept of “object-choice” was first delineated by Freud in his seminal differentiation between two types of choice of love-object: the anaclitic and the narcissistic. Although this distinction has given rise to some important and interesting ideas, it has been difficult to distinguish the anaclitic from the narcissistic object-choice, because it appears that every object-choice is based on and modeled after one's primary relationship(s), whether one seeks out the object or seeks to become the object. The polarities of anaclitic versus narcissistic object-choice and internalization versus object relations oversimplify and distort the complex developmental process of object-choice beset with conflicts, compromises, and active structuring and restructuring. We suggest and clinically illustrate the following organizing principle: In object-choice, there is an attempt to repeat and/or rework conflictual aspects of the original objects and the self. That is, object-choice can be examined in terms of the degree of repetitive torment versus reworking and growth. It is hoped that this conceptualization may prove helpful to psychoanalytic thinkers in their study of the vicissitudes of object-choice and as one overall measure of health versus pathology.

Huntington

William Ryan   Is in private practice in Huntington, NY. Recently he co-authored a book, Love Blocks (Viking 1990), and is working currently on another about self-forgiveness.

Much is being learned about the treatment of adult children of alcoholics (ACOAs) from the areas of self-help psychology, the psychology of addictive families, and group psychotherapy. There is a dearth of material in the psychoanalytic literature. Yet it seems vital for psychoanalysis to contribute the powerful insights it has to offer in understanding in greater depth the character formation and treatment of ACOAs. This article attempts to contribute in the ara of treatment strategies. Two cardinal strategies have been selected for elaboration in this article. One is the process of working through the denial and shame. The other is the focus on the parents as inadequate objects for emotional supplies, and the working through of the inner sadness of the resultant deprivation.

Introduction

One of the gratuitous rewards of longevity is to be witness to dramatic change. That change was brought about largely by the women's movement. And, it is particularly gratifying that, in contrast to so much of what has happened in the last half century, this can be labeled as progress. It was a matter of personal good fortune that I had, in the last 20 years, the opportunity to be a close observer and somewhat of an activist. Although endpoint is still a great distance away, for me it provided an exception to Pastor Reinhold Niebuhr's caution that anything really worthwhile cannot be accomplished in one lifetime. And happiness is compounded as I see that my own very conservative profession is fast catching up with this salutary sea change. The “helping professions,” along with theologians and religionists, although kicking and screaming, are now confronting both sickness and sin in the illumination largely provided by others. Our professions are becoming gender integrated.

NOTE: This is Part I of a three-part essay that reviews six books on psychoanalysis and women.

Hans Strupp   Is Distinguished Professor of Psychology, Vanderbilt University, Nashville, TN. His primary interest is psychotherapy research.

This is a superb book that bears vivid testimony to the renascence of psychoanalysis in West Germany after a long period of stagnation directly traceable to its persecution and virtual extinction during the period of the Third Reich. By 1938, Freud, aging and mortally ill, had fled to England and many of his most creative followers had emigrated to the U.S., where psychoanalysis began to flourish in unprecedented fashion. As has now been documented, psychoanalysis continued a certain underground existence in Hitler's Germany, but in a real sense it had expired. Following the war, psychoanalysis in Germany was slow to revive. In the U.S., psychoanalysis had largely become institutionalized and ritualized; it had withdrawn to its quasireligious training institutes and lost contact with empirical research. Many psychoanalytic journals had become archaic and baroque. Thus one might also expect to encounter these trends in the writings of Thomä and Kächele.

Instead, these two authors present a critical and refreshing treatise of psychoanalysis at its best. Not only are Thomä and Kächele thoroughly conversant with the contemporary literature, but, unlike most American authors, they have forged a connection with empirical research. At the University of Ulm (West Germany) they have created one of the foremost psychoanalytic research centers and have themselves become leaders in empirical research. Their approach is a far cry from the venerated method of clinical observation in the consulting room.

This collection of the letters of James and Alix Strachey will capture the interest of students of psychology, psychiatry, and psychoanalysis, not only because of the Strachey's historical significance as the translators of the English Standard Edition of Freud's complete works, but also because of its insights into the personalities of early figures in psychoanalysis as well as into the sociocultural milieus of Berlin and London in the 1920s. Shortly after their marriage in 1920, this brilliant but troubled young couple from the highly advantaged aristocratic world of Cambridge and the fashionable society of Bloomsbury, journeyed to Vienna where each was analyzed by Freud. Not too long after their analyses began, Freud asked James Strachey to translate some of his papers into English. Alix Strachey collaborated on some of these translations and the couple subsequently became the officially sanctioned translators of all of Freud's works.

They became, as well, psychoanalysts and principal figures in the British Psychoanalytic Society which had been founded several years earlier by Ernest Jones. James Strachey was Donald Winnicott's first analyst during a 10-year analysis and the author of an original and important paper on the curative role of mutative interpretations (made in light of transference). Alix Strachey later completed English translations of the works of Karl Abraham and Otto Fenichel, and during her stay in Berlin she cultivated an intimate friendship with Melanie Klein who, like Alix, was in analysis with Abraham. Alix translated all of Klein's first papers into English and helped her develop her first papers on child analysis. A Major Factor in Some Treatment Impasses

Herbert Strean DSW

Herbert Strean

Herbert Strean   Is Director of the New York Center for Psychoanalytic Training; Distinguished Professor Emeritus, Rutgers University; and Editor-in-Chief of Current Issues in Psychoanalytic Practice. Author of 25 books and 100 professional articles, his most recent work includes Behind the Couch: Revelations of Psychoanalyst; Resolving Resistances in Psychotherapy; and Our Wish to Kill: The Murder in All of Our Hearts. Although it has been well established that psychoanalytic candidates can suffer from many of the same unresolved conflicts as their patients, often colluding with them to create treatment impasses, the role of the supervisor in this collusion has been muchless documented.

In this article, an attempt is made to demonstrate how the psychoanalytic supervisor's conflicts can complement and compoundthe difficulties of the candidate and of his or her patient. As a consequence, all three parties can unconsciously collude to createa therapeutic impasse. Three case examples are presented to illustrate the supervisor's contribution to treatment impasses, particularly how he or she colludes with the candidate and the patient. It is suggested that when the supervisor becomes aware of his or her own role in the treatment impasse, resistances and counter-resistances in the psychoanalytic treatment situation can move toward resolution.

The recent publicized attention to Freud's abandonment of the seduction trauma theory to account for neurosogenesis has led to considerable re-examination of numerous interrelated psychoanalytic issues. Freud's complemental series principle serves to reconcile the apparent polarities: memory and fantasy, early experience and libidinal fixations, historical truth and psychic reality, and interpersonal and intrapsychic representations. I aim to show that although the importance of seduction and other trauma has never been rejected by psychoanalysis, the significance of such early experience has undergone historically important vicissitudes in our consideration of its place in our evolving and expanding theoretical and clinical contexts.

Jill Gardner

Jill Gardner   Is the Director of Adult Outpatient Services at Ravenswood Hospital Community Mental Health Center and in private practice. She is on the faculty of the Postgraduate Education Program at the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis and the Professional Development Program at the University of Chicago School of Social Service Administration. Her interests include the supervisory process and the application of self psychological theory to diverse clinical settings.

This article seeks to establish brief or time-limited psychotherapy as an appropriate domain for the application of self psychology. In this form of treatment, the central focus of the therapist's attention is on the state of the patient's self and on the establishment of a self-selfobject bond as the matrix for change. Whatever the specific precipitants, symptoms, and treatment goals, the therapist attends to ways in which the self is vulnerable, injured, or arrested. The therapist understands symptoms as manifestations of these deficits and enables the patient to establish a selfobject matrix which will facilitate the self's repair and growth. Empathic interpretations which articulate and legitimize the patient's frustrated selfobject longings are continuously offered in an explicit and active way. When conducted along these lines, even a brief treatment relationship may serve to reinstate a previously arrested process of development and structure building. A case example illustrates these principles and provides the reader with an experience in detail of the unfolding therapeutic and developmental process.

The Family Romance

A Developmental-Historical Perspective

Thomas Horner PHD and Elinor Rosenberg MSW

Elinor Rosenberg

Elinor Rosenberg   Is a senior supervising social worker in the Department of Psychiatry at the University of Michigan, and is on the supervisory and consulting staff of the Center for the Child and the Family at that institution. Her teaching and research activities center on family therapy, the development of the adopted child, and family dynamics relating to adoption.

The concept of family romance has played a prominent role in psychodynamic conceptualizations of development. In many quarters it is viewed as a naturally occurring phenomenon centering on the emerging identity and narcissistic re-equilibrations of the late latency-aged child heading toward the pubertal crest of adolescent ego development. This essay examines the concept of family romance from the perspective of childhood in Western sociocultural history. It traces the child's emerging awareness of kinship structures and bonds, and explores its roots as a clinically significant phenomenon. Focusing on the special but numerous cases throughout history of uprooted (displaced, fostered, or adopted) children, we conclude that the family romances of both rooted and uprooted children derive from an interplay of sociocultural dynamics with the self-representational dynamics of the pre-pubertal child, and that family romances in their elaborated forms are specific to the (a) uprooted child's unique tasks of blending emerging kinship knowledge with the realities and associated affects of being a displaced person, and (b) the rooted child's coming to terms with societal factors relating to child loss, theft, and abandonment.

Transference as a Shared (Illusionary) Reality

Technical Implications for Certain Types of Eroticized Treatment Relationships

Derek Stern PHD

Derek Stern

Derek Stern   Is Chief of Psychology at Lake Shore Hospital in Manchester, NH and has a private practice in Exeter, NH. He is a graduate of the Austen Riggs Center's Fellowship program, training there from 1982–1986.

The phenomenon of patient-therapist sexual liaisons raises important questions of technique, ethics, and theory. In this article, some aspects of both the patient's and therapist's vulnerabilities in dealing with an eroticized transference are illustrated by a case report of a patient who is a probable incest victim and who is convinced that a sexual liaison will be the inevitable outcome of her therapy. The thesis developed is that unlike other transference phenomena, the wish for sexual contact, as a repetition of past trauma, requires the analyst to rupture the area of illusion in the transference and foreclose the fantasy in favor of promoting boundary formation and repairing ego deficits. The key to introducing a parameter such as this is understanding from moment to moment the patient's participation in the play-like, illusionary qualities of the transference.

What's in a Name? Psychoanalytic Considerations on Children of Famous Parents

Danielle Knafo PHD

Danielle Knafo

Danielle Knafo   Is a faculty member at Eugene Lang College, the New School for Social Research, New York. She is in private practice in New York City. Her primary interest is psychoanalysis and creativity, a topic on which she has lectured and written extensively, including a forthcoming book consisting of an applied psychoanalytic study of the artist, Egon Schiele.

This article explores the dynamics of children who grow up with famous or infamous parents. Biographies and autobiographies, 16 semistructured interview, and two clinical case studies were drawn on to comprehend the major issues faced by celebrity offspring. The data reveal a paradoxical picture of privilege and neglect that creates narcissistic wounds, ambivalent and magical identifications, and difficulties moving out of the famous parent's shadow in order to establish a mature sense of identity. Professional competition and love relationships take different routes, depending on whether the celebrity is the same-sex or opposite-sex parent. Children of infamous parents experience an added need to defend and justify their parents’ deeds. Implications for treatment and countertransference precautions are discussed.
